div.p: SQL uses three-valued logic: TRUE, FALSE, and NULL (unknown). Every comparison can result in NULL, not just TRUE or FALSE. NULL = NULL evaluates to NULL (not TRUE!) — use IS NULL instead.

Practical Impact: • WHERE status = 'active' OR premium = TRUE If status is NULL and premium is FALSE: → (NULL) OR (FALSE) = NULL → row is SKIPPED! This is why WHERE x IS NULL is required for NULL checks.

g.text: T ∧ T = T | T ∧ F = F | T ∧ N = N
F ∧ T = F | F ∧ F = F | F ∧ N = F
N ∧ T = N | N ∧ F = F | N ∧ N = N
T ∨ T = T | T ∨ F = T | T ∨ N = T
F ∨ T = T | F ∨ F = F | F ∨ N = N
N ∨ T = T | N ∨ F = N | N ∨ N = N
WHERE x IS NULL → checks for NULL
NULL = NULL evaluates to NULL (unknown), not TRUE
